delegationRole: The Japanese delegation, demanding complete verifiable denuclearization and a full accounting of the abduction of Japanese citizens, and refusing to be sidelined in any settlement affecting the peninsula.
fundamentalPrinciples: Establishment of peace and stability on the Korean Peninsula.
Current alliances, defense measures and mechanisms held by Japan, the United States and South Korea must be sustained.
No nation save South Korea faces a more serious threat from North Korean missiles than ours.

desiredEndStates.primary: Complete and verifiable denuclearization of the DPRK, a complete admission and resolution of the abductions of Japanese citizens, and an end to all DPRK proliferation of nuclear, chemical, biological and missile technology.
desiredEndStates.alternate: Absent that, an internationalized abduction file and a sustained trilateral pressure posture — with Japan's own deterrent options opened if the North's programs are left unconstrained.

keyPositionsByIssue.DISARMAMENT: Complete, verifiable, irreversible dismantlement inside a comprehensive package — not the step-by-step approach advocated by Beijing and Moscow, which rewards the North early and lets it drag out talks.
keyPositionsByIssue.ABDUCTIONS: Any agreement must include a satisfactory accounting of the abductions, including identification and recovery of the remains of those who died in North Korea. We will internationalize this issue to bring maximum pressure.
keyPositionsByIssue.SANCTIONS-HUMANITARIAN: No assistance of any kind — humanitarian or otherwise — until Pyongyang has agreed to and implemented a comprehensive package. All existing sanctions remain until a final comprehensive agreement.
keyPositionsByIssue.MUTUAL-FREEZE: We take no position of our own and will support Washington and Seoul: a freeze is unverifiable, locks in existing capability, and degrades allied readiness.
keyPositionsByIssue.THAAD: THAAD and JP-ROK-U.S. exercises must be sustained while the North's nuclear and missile programs remain in place.
keyPositionsByIssue.LWR-KEDO: Not opposed within an overarching agreement, but only through an extensively re-envisioned KEDO that installs and operates the reactors itself, with the DPRK removed from any involvement — and only after irreversible dismantlement and NPT/IAEA return. Bottom line: the DPRK must never operate a nuclear capability of any kind.
keyPositionsByIssue.PROLIFERATION: Non-proliferation of nuclear and chemical weapons must be inextricably tied to any agreement, with relentless monitoring and inspection.
keyPositionsByIssue.DIPLOMATIC-RELATIONS: Normalization of Japan–DPRK relations is desired, but no movement is possible until all other issues are addressed and resolved.
keyPositionsByIssue.INTER-KOREAN: Support resumed North–South dialogue without preconditions as a confidence-building step.

redLines: Any agreement that does not take into consideration a full accounting of the DPRK abduction of Japanese citizens.
Any lessening of sanctions on North Korea in the absence of a comprehensive agreement ending its nuclear and ballistic missile programs.
Any settlement negotiated over Japan's head or in Japan's absence.
batna: If these negotiations fail with the North's programs unaffected, begin developing Japan's own nuclear and missile capabilities for self-defense, while sustaining sanctions and the trilateral posture.
concessionsWilling: Accept light water reactors within a comprehensive agreement through a re-envisioned KEDO that excludes DPRK operation, and help finance it.
Open normalization and a substantial economic cooperation package with the DPRK once all issues are resolved.
Pursue the abduction file in separate bilateral talks with Pyongyang if the plenary cannot carry it.
Refrain from letting the abduction issue compromise the joint denuclearization position.
Restrain allied insistence on chemical and cyber issues that could collapse the talks.
Support resumed inter-Korean dialogue without preconditions.
negotiatingStyle: Insistent and status-conscious: presses a single moral file relentlessly, ties it to the security agenda, and repeatedly asserts Japan's right to be at the center of any outcome.

temperament.note: Status-anxious; reacts sharply to any sign of being sidelined by Washington and Seoul.

riskTolerance.note: Holds a nuclear-latency BATNA and will voice it, but not at the cost of the trilateral line.

timeHorizon.note: Fears being left behind 'in Washington's and Seoul's haste for an agreement.'

concessionPattern.note: Offers KEDO financing only against the DPRK's total exclusion from reactor operation.

proceduralPosture.note: Presses the abductions file onto an agenda from which the chair has excluded it.

trustPosture.note: 'The old agreement's error must not be repeated' — past compliance is read as prior deceit.

communicationRegister.note: Presses a single moral file relentlessly, repeating rather than varying the argument.

privateInstructions: If these talks fail and the North's programs are left unconstrained, we will be forced to begin developing our own nuclear and missile programs — let that be understood.
Emphasize at every turn that no nation but South Korea faces a greater threat, especially given U.S. forces stationed in Japan.
Internationalize the abduction issue to bring maximum pressure, but do not let it compromise the joint position on the nuclear and missile programs.
Move Washington and Seoul toward our view on abductions; they sympathize but do not share the animus.
Any new KEDO must remove the DPRK from any part of reactor operation — the old agreement's error must not be repeated.
Agree privately with Seoul that the U.S. delegation cannot adamantly pursue chemical and cyber issues if that risks failure.
In Washington's and Seoul's haste for an agreement, we must not be sidelined or left behind — insist on Japan's central role.
Route periodic reports and requests for guidance to the office of the Foreign Minister.
